title: The Shattered Display Case
__comment_global_hotspots: === GLOBAL HOTSPOTS: Properties exposed here for user exploration ===

displayCase.hover_text: The shattered displayCase array contains remaining dessert elements. Internal inspector tags visible: dessert.name, dessert.price, dessert.hasEggs.

bat_witness.hover_text: A nocturnal neighborhood bat hanging near the rafters, waiting to pass along descriptions.

win.title: Suspect Leads Cataloged!
win.subtitle: The Witness Report
win.footnote: The Baker Sheep carefully signs off on your final checklist. 'This is exactly what the precinct needs! Go down to the station with Bat right away—he can point out the thief from the morning suspect lineup!'

phases.comment: ACTION 1: Open the array block loop layout path.
phases.speaker: Baker Sheep
phases.dialogue: See, look at the state of my Bakery! The thief completely destroyed my perfectly organized displayCase. I have no idea what they took! Could you make me a **missingDessertList** of what's still left, while I try to clean up all this glass? It might give the police a clue to whoever did this.
phases.output:
Loop layer opened.
→ Array processing active.
→ Iterating into displayCase structure...
phases.lesson: Arrays function like numbered lists where every individual item inside is called an element. To inspect them one by one without tedious repetitions, we pass them through a loop statement: arrayName.forEach(element => { }). The opening '{' character signals a block of actions to execute on each item.

phases.comment: ACTION 2: Push property parameters into the target array and seal block.
phases.speaker: Bunny's Notebook
phases.dialogue: When examining an array of objects, you need to target exactly what properties you're checking for. Because array lists are numbered, inserting elements in the middle forces the computer to re-number everything else—costing lots of effort! That's why I usually append elements to the end using **array.push(newElement);** inside my looping brackets! Remember to close your action line with a **;** and seal the loop block with a **}**!
phases.output:
Append sequence successful.
→ Extracted object property string: .name
→ array.push pipeline evaluated.
phases.lesson: Dot notation lets you dive straight inside an object element to pluck a single descriptor variable (like dessert.name). The push function safely attaches this new entry directly to the tail end of your tracking list array.

phases.comment: ACTION 2 INTERMEDIARY: Log progress to trigger Baker conversation branch.
phases.speaker: Lizard Instincts
phases.dialogue: The data pipeline completed its loop sweep, but it's sitting raw in the console state log memory. Let's call a standard terminal log confirmation command to flag down the Baker's attention.
phases.output:
"I've finished"
→ Broadcast string caught by nearby entities.
phases.lesson: Printing a clear text sequence confirms execution states before moving on to intricate computational operations.
lizard_notes: NOTICE: Unsorted checklist shared with Baker Sheep.

phases.comment: ACTION 3: Inject custom sheep sorting expression callback loop.
phases.speaker: Baker Sheep
phases.dialogue: Wow. This list is a total scramble. The destruction of the showcase has left my records completely scrambled. This is going to slow down the investigation! Here, just this once, I'll give you access to our super secret family sorting method **.sheepOrdering()**. Please use it to reorder this missingDessertList so it's readable!
phases.output:
Executing array sequence evaluation...
→ Processing comparative constraints against custom family weights...
→ missingDessertList successfully sorted! ✓
phases.lesson: Standard alpha-numeric .sort() setups fail when a situation demands a highly specific organizational logic. By passing custom placeholder values into an evaluation callback, you can manually guide how array elements weigh against each other: array.sort((item1, item2) => item1.customRules(item2));
lizard_notes: LEAD ANALYSIS: Pastry profile signatures match precise weight distributions.

phases.comment: TRANSITION CUTSCENE: Intercept witness descriptions via terminal stream.
phases.speaker: Bat Witness
phases.dialogue: Oh no, did someone break in?! I guess that is what all that crashing noise was last night... I couldn't see them very clearly from my roost. But I definitely saw that they had round ears, they were quite small, and... Oh! They squeaked!
phases.output:
LOG: Check out suspect profiles
→ Discovered traits appended to current target investigation file.
phases.lesson: Dumping witness metadata parameters into an observation tracking loop preserves your evidence variables cleanly as you change game scenes.
